content.method: Longitudinal cohort tracking using linked administrative education and tax/labor-market records, applying regression discontinuity around admission cutoffs to isolate causal effects of track placement from selection bias, following students from grade 5/7 entry through early-career earnings.
content.question: Do short-term achievement gains for students selected into 6/8-year gimnázium programs translate into long-term labor-market earnings or career outcomes, or do they primarily reflect test-score inflation and peer-composition effects without persistent human-capital gains?
content.why_it_matters: The entire policy debate rests on trading off documented short-term achievement gains for selected students against system-wide equity costs. If long-term individual benefits are minimal, the case for preserving any selective track (or resisting reform) weakens substantially; if benefits are substantial and durable, quality-investment-only approaches (TP-live-t4) undervalue what tracks provide to admitted students.
